g.data-narration: Requirements Engineering forms the foundation of successful software development, bridging stakeholder needs with technical solutions.
Requirements Engineering is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, documenting, validating, and managing stakeholder needs.
Poor requirements account for nearly 40% of software project failures, making RE critical for project success.
g.text: Effective RE reduces development time, minimizes rework, decreases costs,

g.data-narration: There are two main types of requirements: functional specify what the system does, non-functional specify how it performs.
Functional requirements define system behaviors, features, inputs, outputs, and processing logic from the user's perspective.
g.text: Define what the system should do - behaviors, features, functions
"The system shall allow students to search books by title, author, or ISBN"

g.data-narration: Non-functional requirements specify quality attributes like performance, security, reliability, usability, and maintainability.
g.text: Define how the system should perform - quality attributes and constraints

g.data-narration: The Requirements Engineering process has five phases: Elicitation, Analysis, Specification, Validation, and Management.
g.text: Five interconnected phases for managing requirements

g.data-narration: Elicitation discovers requirements from stakeholders; Analysis studies them to find inconsistencies, gaps, and prioritizes for feasibility.
Specification documents requirements in SRS; Validation ensures they accurately represent stakeholder needs and are testable.
g.text: Create detailed SRS document per IEEE 830

g.data-narration: Requirements Management handles changes throughout the lifecycle, including version control, traceability, and conflict resolution.
g.text: Ongoing process of handling changes throughout project lifecycle

g.data-narration: Key elicitation techniques include interviews, questionnaires, observation, prototyping, and Joint Application Development.
The SRS document follows IEEE 830 standard with three main sections: Introduction, Overall Description, and Specific Requirements.
Good requirements are S.M.A.R.T: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound for testability.
g.text: Requirements must be testable and measurable

g.data-narration: A good requirement is testable: vague 'user-friendly' becomes specific measurable criteria for validation.
Remember: RE is critical for success, functional vs non-functional types, five-phase process, S.M.A.R.T requirements.
g.text: Gathering: Interviews, Questionnaires, Observation, Prototyping, JAD
